disease.name: Lipoma
disease.note: Benign soft tissue tumor of adipose tissue. Soft, mobile, painless. Usually on trunk, neck, and extremities. In clinical practice in Kenya, these are frequently excised for cosmetic reasons or diagnostic uncertainty. Always consider liposarcoma for large (>10 cm), deep, or rapidly growing lesions. The adage 'a lipoma is a lipoma unless it is not' — any atypical feature warrants MRI before excision.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Rapid growth/atypical features -> SEVERE (rule out liposarcoma); classic lipoma -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: How long have you had this lump?
Has it changed size recently?
Is it painful?
Do you have any other similar lumps elsewhere?
Has anyone in your family had similar lumps?
Where exactly is the lump located?
Do you feel any numbness or tingling in the area?
Has it ever changed color?
Does it interfere with movement or clothing?
Have you had any surgery to remove a lump before?
Do you have any weight loss or other symptoms?
Is the lump in the muscle or under the skin?
Do you have any chronic illnesses like diabetes?
Does it feel soft or hard when you touch it?
Have you noticed it growing faster recently?
How long has this swelling been present? Has it changed recently?
Is the lump painful or tender? Does it cause any tingling or numbness?
Do you have similar lumps elsewhere on your body?
Has anyone in your family had similar fatty lumps?
Do you have a history of cancer (sarcoma, breast, colon, thyroid)?
Is the lump causing any functional limitation or difficulty moving a joint?
Have you unintentionally lost weight in the past 6 months?
Do you have diabetes, obesity, or high cholesterol?
Have you had any surgeries or injuries at this site in the past?
Are you taking any medications (steroids, antiretroviral therapy)?
Does the lump change size with your weight changes?
Have you had any imaging (ultrasound, CT, MRI) of this lump before?
What is your main concern: cosmetic, functional, or fear of cancer?
For women: is the lump related to your menstrual cycle?
Do you drink alcohol regularly and heavily?
Is the overlying skin normal in color and temperature?
Does the lump move freely when you push it?
Have you had any trauma to this area in the past?
Do you have any history of lipodystrophy or fat redistribution?
Has the lump ever been red, warm, or increased in size suddenly?

examination_findings.finding: Soft, rubbery consistency
Well-circumscribed borders
Mobile under the skin
Lobulated surface
No overlying skin changes
Negative transillumination (does not light up)
Non-tender on palpation
Size ranging from 1-10 cm (usually)
Located in subcutaneous plane
Deep or intramuscular (less common)
Multiple lesions in familial cases
Positive slip sign (margins slip under fingers)
Measure lump in three dimensions (cm)
Palpate consistency: soft, doughy, rubbery, firm, or hard
Assess mobility over underlying muscle fascia
Check for the slip sign (lump slips away when edge is pressed)
Inspect overlying skin for tethering, punctum, or color change
Transilluminate the mass in a dark room
Palpate for a well-defined edge (capsule)
Check for tenderness on deep palpation
Perform neurologic exam distal to the mass
Assess range of motion of adjacent joints
Inspect for similar lumps elsewhere on the body
Check for signs of lipodystrophy (focal fat loss, buffalo hump)
Palpate regional lymph nodes
Evaluate lobulation vs smooth surface
Check skin temperature compared to contralateral side
Assess lump with muscle contracted vs relaxed
Check for visible pulsation or bruit
Assess with patient sitting and standing for postural change
Check for positive dimple sign (skin dimples when mass compressed laterally)
Document asymmetry compared with contralateral side

bedside_scores.name: Clinical Suspicion Score for Liposarcoma
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=3 requires MRI for further characterization before excision.

subtypes.name: Superficial Lipoma
subtypes.management: Reassurance and observation. Excision if symptomatic, growing, cosmetic concern, or diagnostic uncertainty. Simple excision under LA.

subtypes.name: Deep or Atypical Lipomatous Tumor
subtypes.management: MRI for characterization. Excision with histology. Wide local excision if atypical lipomatous tumor/well-differentiated liposarcoma. Long-term follow-up.

subtypes.name: Giant Lipoma (>10 cm)
subtypes.management: MRI preoperatively. Excision under GA. Drain dead space. Higher risk of seroma and recurrence.

subtypes.name: Intramuscular/Intermuscular Lipoma
subtypes.management: MRI for diagnosis and surgical planning. Excision with careful hemostasis. Higher neurological risk. Specialist referral.

subtypes.name: Madelung Disease (Multiple Symmetric Lipomatosis)
subtypes.management: Liposuction or excision for symptomatic lesions. Alcohol cessation to prevent progression. Airway assessment.

imaging.name: Ultrasound Soft Tissue
imaging.indication: confirm fatty nature, rule out complex features

imaging.name: MRI with contrast
imaging.indication: if deep, large, or suspicious features

management.protocolReference: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue Tumors

excision.description: Simple excision under local or general anesthesia
excision.step3: Make incision along skin creases (elliptical or linear)
excision.step5: Deliver the lipoma using pressure from opposite side
excision.step6: Blunt and sharp dissection to free capsule
excision.step7: Remove in toto (if fragmented, ensure complete removal)
excision.step9: Close dead space with absorbable sutures

pathways.treatment: Reassurance. Excision under LA if symptomatic or cosmetic. No further follow-up needed if histology confirms lipoma.
MRI. Excision with histology. Refer to sarcoma MDT. Wide excision if atypical lipomatous tumor.

operative_templates.incision: Linear or elliptical over the mass
steps: Incision through skin and subcutaneous tissue
Bluntly dissect around capsule with scissors or finger
Clamp and ligate any pedicle vessels
Remove intact and send for histology
Close dead space with interrupted absorbable sutures
Close skin with subcuticular 3-0 or 4-0 Monocryl

dischargeCriteria: Patient informed about histology result follow-up

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: A lipoma is a benign (non-cancerous) growth of fat cells under the skin. It is not dangerous and does not turn into cancer. It may be removed if it bothers you or for cosmetic reasons.
patient_education.warningSigns: Rapid growth, pain, hardness, or change in appearance of the lump.
patient_education.activity: Normal activities immediately after surgery. Avoid heavy lifting or strenuous activity for 1 week.
patient_education.medication: Paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain if needed after excision.
patient_education.followUp: Return for histology result review in 7-14 days.

pathophysiologyDetail: A lipoma is a benign mesenchymal tumor composed of mature adipocytes, representing the most common soft tissue tumor in adults, with a lifetime incidence of approximately 1%. The pathogenesis involves a spectrum of genetic alterations: approximately 60-70% of solitary lipomas harbor rearrangements of chromosomal region 12q13-15, involving the HMGA2 (high mobility group AT-hook 2) gene, which encodes an architectural transcription factor that regulates adipocyte differentiation and proliferation. Other lipomas show deletions of 13q or rearrangements of 6p21. These genetic alterations lead to clonal expansion of preadipocytes that differentiate into mature, non-proliferating adipocytes, resulting in a slowly growing, encapsulated mass of normal-appearing fat that is histologically indistinguishable from surrounding subcutaneous adipose tissue except for its encapsulation and altered regulation of lipogenesis.

Clinically, lipomas present as soft, painless, slow-growing subcutaneous masses that are freely mobile over the underlying muscle fascia. They most commonly occur on the trunk, proximal extremities, neck, and forearms. Multiple lipomas (lipomatosis) may be associated with familial syndromes including familial multiple lipomatosis (autosomal dominant, onset in 3rd-4th decade), Dercum disease (adiposis dolorosa — painful lipomas with obesity, weakness, and psychiatric disturbance), Proteus syndrome, and Cowden syndrome (PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome). In the Kenyan context, patients frequently present with large, neglected lipomas that have grown over years of delayed healthcare seeking, driven by the benign nature of the lesion and the cost of surgical consultation. Large lipomas (>10 cm) may cause mechanical symptoms (pressure on nerves causing paresthesias, restriction of joint movement, discomfort with clothing) and raise cosmetic concerns. The clinical differential diagnosis includes sebaceous cyst (attached to skin, central punctum), lymphadenopathy (firm, non-mobile), neurofibroma (associated with skin changes, family history), and liposarcoma (rapid growth, deep location, size >5 cm, firm consistency, heterogeneous on imaging).

landmarkTrials.trial: Liposuction vs Excision for Lipoma: Systematic Review
landmarkTrials.intervention: Liposuction-assisted lipoma excision vs open surgical excision
landmarkTrials.outcome: Liposuction: smaller scar, faster recovery, lower recurrence (3% vs 8%), higher patient satisfaction; open excision: more complete removal, lower seroma rate, better for large lipomas
landmarkTrials.impact: Both techniques are effective; choice depends on size, location, and patient preference
landmarkTrials.reference: Lee SY, et al. Comparison of liposuction versus open excision for lipoma. J Plast Reconstr Aesthet Surg. 2018;71(6):839-846.

landmarkTrials.population: Case series of 35 patients with small lipomas (<3 cm)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Complete resolution in 60%, partial reduction in 25%, no response in 15% at 12 weeks
landmarkTrials.impact: Non-surgical option for small lipomas in patients who decline surgery; multiple injections may be needed
landmarkTrials.reference: Ribeiro RC, et al. Intralesional triamcinolone for treatment of lipomas. Dermatol Surg. 2014;40(8):876-881.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: DIAGNOSE -> ASSESS -> DECIDE -> EXCISE. DIAGNOSE: The diagnosis of lipoma is primarily clinical. Typical features: soft, doughy or rubbery consistency, well-circumscribed, lobulated, freely mobile over deeper structures, non-tender, overlying skin normal (no tethering, no punctum). Transillumination is positive (lipomas transilluminate due to homogeneous fat density). The 'slip sign' is characteristic: when pressure is applied at the edge of the mass, it slips away from the examining finger. If any feature is atypical (firm consistency, rapid growth, size >10 cm, deep fixation, pain, overlying skin changes), imaging is indicated. Ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality: a lipoma appears as a well-defined, elliptical, hyperechoic (or isoechoic relative to subcutaneous fat) mass with no internal vascularity on Doppler, and no posterior shadowing. If ultrasound is equivocal or the mass is deep to the fascia, MRI shows characteristic fat signal (hyperintense on T1, hypointense on fat-suppressed sequences) and no enhancement. CT shows uniform fat density (-65 to -120 Hounsfield units). ASSESS: Document size (three dimensions), location (superficial vs deep to fascia), proximity to neurovascular structures, functional impairment (joint range of motion, nerve compression), cosmetic concern, and growth rate over time. Indications for excision: symptomatic (pain, pressure, functional limitation), cosmetic concern, rapid growth (suspicious for liposarcoma), size >5 cm with atypical features, or patient request. Asymptomatic lipomas with typical features do not require excision or follow-up. DECIDE: For small lipomas (<3 cm) with typical features in low-risk locations, no treatment is needed. For symptomatic or cosmetically concerning lipomas, options include: open excision (gold standard, complete removal possible), minimal excision technique (small incision with lipoma extrusion), liposuction (smaller scar, better cosmesis, higher recurrence), or intralesional steroid injection (small lipomas only, variable response). EXCISE: For open excision, plan the incision along skin crease lines. Infiltrate with lidocaine 1% with epinephrine. Incise down to the capsule, dissect circumferentially around the capsule using blunt dissection (Metzenbaum scissors or finger dissection), deliver the intact lipoma, achieve hemostasis, close in layers, and apply a pressure dressing. The key principle: the entire capsule must be removed to prevent recurrence. A 'piecemeal' removal (fragmentation and suction extraction) leaves capsular remnants and carries a 5-15% recurrence rate.

managementPearls.pearl: Lipomas do NOT require excision unless symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome. Patients can be reassured of the benign nature and discharged. Rapid growth, size >10 cm, deep location, or new pain should prompt imaging and consideration of liposarcoma.

The minimal excision technique for small lipomas: make a 3-5 mm incision through the skin and capsule, then apply pressure around the lipoma to express it through the small opening ('squeeze technique'). The lipoma extrudes through the incision, and the capsule follows. This leaves a scar of only 3-5 mm. However, incomplete extrusion with capsule remnant increases recurrence risk.

In the Kenyan context, patients often present with large neglected lipomas (>10 cm) that have grown over decades. These require excision under regional or general anesthesia, not local. A planned 4-6 cm incision with careful dissection of the capsule is required. The dead space after removing a large lipoma should be closed with deep absorbable sutures to prevent seroma formation.

Atypical lipomatous tumor/well-differentiated liposarcoma can mimic a simple lipoma. Suspicious features: size >10 cm, deep to fascia (retroperitoneal, intramuscular), rapid growth, heterogeneous on imaging (thick septa, nodular foci, contrast enhancement), or age >60 years. ANY lesion with these features requires preoperative MRI or core needle biopsy before excision.

operativeNuances.nuance: When excising a lipoma, the key step is to identify the correct plane between the lipoma capsule and the surrounding normal fat. This plane is avascular and can be developed with gentle blunt dissection using Metzenbaum scissors or a Kittner dissector. Once the capsule is identified, the lipoma shells out easily. The most common error is starting the dissection within the capsule (entering the lipoma substance) rather than outside it, which fragments the lipoma and makes complete removal difficult.

For large lipomas of the upper back and posterior neck (interscapular region), these are often poorly encapsulated and infiltrate surrounding tissues (so-called 'interscapular lipoma of the elderly'). They are less distinct from surrounding fat and require wider excision to ensure complete removal. Recurrence in this area is higher (15-25%) due to incomplete removal.

prognosis.recurrenceAfterCompleteExcision: 1-2% if entire capsule removed; 5-15% if fragmented removal or piecemeal excision
prognosis.malignantTransformation: Essentially zero for typical subcutaneous lipoma; well-differentiated liposarcoma is a separate entity, not malignant transformation
prognosis.complicationRate: Seroma: 3-5%; hematoma: 2-3%; wound infection: 1-3%; hypertrophic scar: variable
prognosis.kenyanConsiderations: Patients often present with very large neglected lipomas requiring specialized excision; recurrence from incomplete removal at district hospitals is common; training in proper capsule identification is key
